class_schedule.topic: Introduction to HRM and Strategic Human Resource Management
learning_objectives: Define HRM and its strategic importance to organizational success
Identify the key functions of HRM: staffing, development, compensation, relations
Explain how HR strategy aligns with business strategy

lecture_notes.introduction: Human Resource Management is about acquiring, developing, and retaining the talent that drives organizational performance. In a knowledge economy, people are the most important competitive advantage.
key_concepts: HRM functions: recruitment, selection, training, compensation, employee relations
Strategic HRM: aligning people practices with business objectives
HR as business partner vs. administrative function
Workforce planning and talent pipeline management
Legal and ethical framework for HR decisions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case analysis of how Google's HR practices contribute to its competitive advantage
setup_requirements: Textbook: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.)
SHRM (Society for Human Resource Management) online resources

review.value: Integrating HR practices with strategic planning so human capital drives competitive advantage
Recruitment and selection, training and development, compensation and benefits, employee relations, compliance

learning_objectives: Conduct a job analysis and write job descriptions and specifications
Compare internal and external recruitment sources
Evaluate selection methods for validity and reliability

lecture_notes.introduction: Getting the right people in the right roles is the foundation of organizational performance. A systematic approach to recruitment and selection reduces costly hiring mistakes.
key_concepts: Job analysis methods: observation, interview, questionnaire, diary
Job description vs. job specification
Recruitment sources: job boards, referrals, campus, agencies, social media
Selection tools: interviews, tests, assessment centers, background checks
Validity (predictive, content, construct) and reliability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing a job description and designing a structured interview for a marketing manager position

review.value: Asking all candidates the same predetermined questions with standardized rating scales; higher validity than unstructured
The degree to which a selection tool predicts future job performance; the gold standard for selection methods

learning_objectives: Design a training program using the ADDIE model
Address common rating errors and biases
readings: Kirkpatrick's Four Levels of Training Evaluation

lecture_notes.introduction: Organizations that invest in developing their people outperform those that do not. Effective training and fair performance management are essential for engagement and retention.
key_concepts: ADDIE model: Analysis, Design, Development, Implementation, Evaluation
Training methods: on-the-job, classroom, e-learning, simulation
Kirkpatrick evaluation: reaction, learning, behavior, results
Performance appraisal methods: 360-degree, MBO, BARS, ranking
Rating errors: halo, leniency, central tendency, recency
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a training needs assessment and evaluation plan for a customer service team

review.value: Systematic instructional design: Analyze needs, Design objectives, Develop materials, Implement training, Evaluate effectiveness
Performance input from supervisors, peers, subordinates, and self; provides comprehensive perspective but requires trust

learning_objectives: Design a compensation structure using job evaluation methods
Distinguish between direct compensation, benefits, and non-monetary rewards

lecture_notes.introduction: Compensation is more than just a paycheck. A total rewards strategy includes base pay, incentives, benefits, work-life balance, development, and recognition.
key_concepts: Job evaluation methods: point factor, job ranking, classification
Pay structures: grades, ranges, broadbanding
Variable pay: bonuses, commissions, profit sharing, stock options
Benefits: health insurance, retirement plans, PTO, wellness programs
Pay equity, comparable worth, and legal compliance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Point-factor job evaluation exercise to establish internal pay equity across five positions

review.value: Everything an employee values from the employment relationship: compensation, benefits, work-life, performance recognition, development
Equal pay for equal work regardless of gender, race, or other protected characteristics; legally required and ethically essential

learning_objectives: Identify major employment laws and their HR implications
Handle employee discipline using progressive discipline
Manage workplace conflicts and grievance procedures

lecture_notes.introduction: HR professionals must operate within a complex legal framework. Understanding employment law protects both the organization and its employees from unfair treatment.
key_concepts: Title VII, ADA, ADEA, FMLA, FLSA: key provisions
Discrimination types: disparate treatment vs. disparate impact
Progressive discipline: verbal warning, written warning, suspension, termination
At-will employment and its exceptions
Grievance procedures and alternative dispute resolution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Role-play exercise: conducting a disciplinary meeting following proper legal and procedural guidelines

review.value: A neutral policy that disproportionately affects a protected group; even unintentional, it is illegal unless job-related and consistent with business necessity
Escalating consequences for repeated violations: verbal, written, suspension, termination; documents due process

class_schedule.topic: Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and the Future of Work
learning_objectives: Explain the business case for diversity and inclusion
Develop strategies for building inclusive workplaces
Analyze emerging HR trends: remote work, gig economy, AI in HR

lecture_notes.introduction: The workforce is changing. Demographic shifts, technology, and evolving employee expectations are transforming how organizations manage people.
key_concepts: Diversity dimensions: surface-level and deep-level
Inclusion vs. diversity: belonging and psychological safety
Unconscious bias and mitigation strategies
Remote and hybrid work management
AI in HR: recruitment screening, chatbots, predictive analytics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implicit bias awareness exercise and discussion of organizational strategies for building inclusion

review.value: Diversity is representation; inclusion is ensuring everyone can participate fully, contribute, and belong
Belief that one can speak up, take risks, and make mistakes without punishment; essential for inclusion and innovation

questions.question: A company uses an aptitude test for hiring warehouse workers. The test has a passing rate of 80% for male applicants and 50% for female applicants. Using the four-fifths rule, determine if this selection procedure may have adverse impact and explain the next steps.
questions.answer: The four-fifths rule states that the selection rate for a protected group should be at least 80% of the rate for the highest-scoring group. Female rate / Male rate = 50% / 80% = 62.5%. Since 62.5% is less than 80%, the test shows potential adverse impact against female applicants. Next steps: the employer must demonstrate the test is job-related and consistent with business necessity through a validation study. If it cannot be validated, an alternative selection method with less adverse impact should be used.

questions.question: Describe the four levels of Kirkpatrick's training evaluation model and explain why most organizations only evaluate at levels 1 and 2.
questions.answer: Level 1 (Reaction): Did participants like the training? Level 2 (Learning): Did participants acquire the intended knowledge/skills? Level 3 (Behavior): Are participants applying what they learned on the job? Level 4 (Results): Did the training impact business outcomes? Most organizations stop at levels 1-2 because levels 3-4 require time (behavior change takes weeks/months to observe), longitudinal measurement systems, control groups to isolate training effects, and the ability to link individual behavior changes to organizational results, which is methodologically complex and expensive.

rubric.key: Insight into why evaluation difficulty increases

questions.question: Compare pay-for-performance compensation with seniority-based pay. What are the advantages and risks of each approach?
questions.answer: Pay-for-performance: Advantages include motivating high performers, aligning employee efforts with organizational goals, and rewarding results. Risks include fostering unhealthy competition, discouraging collaboration, potential bias in performance measurement, and short-term thinking. Seniority-based pay: Advantages include perceived fairness, retention of experienced employees, ease of administration, and predictability. Risks include rewarding tenure over contribution, demotivating high performers, higher labor costs over time, and difficulty attracting top talent who want performance-based rewards.

rubric.key: Advantages and risks of seniority-based pay
Balanced analysis showing understanding of tradeoffs

questions.question: An employee who has worked for your company for 2 years requests 12 weeks of unpaid leave to care for a seriously ill parent. Under FMLA, is this employee eligible? What conditions must be met?
questions.answer: FMLA eligibility requires: (1) the employer has 50+ employees within 75 miles, (2) the employee has worked for the employer for at least 12 months, and (3) the employee has worked at least 1,250 hours in the past 12 months. If this employee meets the 1,250-hour requirement and the employer is covered, they are eligible for up to 12 weeks of unpaid, job-protected leave to care for a parent with a serious health condition. The employer must maintain health benefits during leave and restore the employee to the same or equivalent position upon return.

rubric.key: Applies criteria to the scenario correctly

major_assignments.title: HR Strategic Plan Development
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive HR strategic plan for a company facing a significant workforce challenge (e.g., high turnover, skills gap, rapid growth, remote workforce transition). The plan must address recruitment, development, compensation, and retention strategies aligned with business objectives.
objectives: Diagnose a workforce challenge using HR analytics
Design HR interventions grounded in theory and best practices
Create measurable objectives and KPIs for HR initiatives
Present a professionally formatted strategic HR plan
deliverables: Workforce analysis with relevant data and metrics
Action plan with timeline, budget, and accountability

criteria: Data-driven analysis with relevant HR metrics
HR interventions align with business strategy
Multiple HR functions integrated into plan
Thorough workforce challenge diagnosis with data
Comprehensive HR strategy aligned with business goals
Realistic implementation plan with measurable KPIs

major_assignments.title: Compensation and Benefits Redesign
major_assignments.description: Analyze and redesign the total rewards package for a mid-size company competing for talent in a tight labor market. Conduct a compensation analysis, benchmark against competitors, and propose a total rewards strategy that attracts, motivates, and retains employees.
objectives: Apply job evaluation methodology to establish internal equity
Conduct competitive pay analysis using market data
Design a total rewards package that balances cost and competitiveness
Address pay equity concerns and legal compliance
deliverables: Job evaluation results with pay structure
Total rewards proposal with cost analysis

criteria: Market data appropriately sourced and applied
Comprehensive rewards package beyond base pay
Aligned with workforce demographics and preferences
Rigorous compensation analysis with market benchmarking
Creative and comprehensive total rewards design
Thorough pay equity audit and compliance review

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of HR concepts and legal knowledge

class_participation.description: Active engagement in case discussions and role-play exercises

major_assignments.description: HR strategic plan and compensation redesign projects

midterm_examination.description: Covers staffing, training, and performance management

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all HR functions and employment law

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. Role-play exercises and in-class discussions are integral and cannot be made up.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments penalized 10% per day late. Presentations must be delivered on scheduled date.
course_policies.collaboration: HR strategic plan may be a team project (2-3 members). Compensation redesign is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All research must be properly attributed. Use of prior semester submissions or purchased papers is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to SHRM resources through university library. Presentation software for strategic plan delivery.
